definition: A view, opinion, or standpoint, often used in doctrinal contexts for right or wrong view.
notes: The project keeps view as the default because it is clear, flexible, and stable across right view, wrong view, self-view, and clinging-to-views compounds. Opinion and belief remain acceptable in explanatory prose when the context is narrower, but translators should not let them displace view in doctrinal formulae or path language. Doctrine is discouraged because it hardens the term into a system of propositions and weakens the practical and appropriative force that many compounds depend on.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and path-factor contexts
In explanatory prose emphasizing a personal or disputable standpoint
context_rules.notes: Use when the line is about holding a viewpoint rather than naming the doctrinal category.

context_rules.context: In prose emphasizing conviction or assent rather than the whole doctrinal family
context_rules.notes: Allowed explanatory alternate, not the default rendering.

context_rules.context: In compounds such as sammā-diṭṭhi, micchā-diṭṭhi, sakkāya-diṭṭhi, and diṭṭhupādāna
context_rules.notes: Keep the headword stable unless the compound entry documents an explicit override.

example_phrases.notes: Most important governed compound in the family.
Shows the same headword in ethical and doctrinal contrast.
Illustrates how the headword extends into appropriation language.

version_notes: Promoted to a major rule-bearing entry so the diṭṭhi family can govern path, self-view, and clinging-to-views compounds consistently.
